coredata.dc:title: Polysulfone/graphene quantum dots composite anion exchange membrane for acid recovery by diffusion dialysis
coredata.dc:creator: Yadav, Vikrant
Raj, Savan K.
Rathod, Nehal H.
Kulshrestha, Vaibhav
coredata.dc:description: The advancement in membrane-based separation technologies offers advantages over traditional ways. Recovery of acid from acidic waste stream is an important issue to be solved by greener route. Diffusion dialysis using anion exchange membranes (AEMs) is an energy-intensive and environment friendly process to be taken forward. Herein, we report the eco-friendly route for chloromethylation of polysulfone using chloromethyl ethyl ether (CMEE). Effect of chloromethyl ethyl ether concentration, reaction temperature, and reaction time are investigated to get maximum tethered chloromethyl group and high yield of product. Series of chloromethylated polysulfone membranes are prepared with different concentrations of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) followed by quaternization using trimethylamine. The prepared membranes are characterized by their water content, transport properties, and stabilities. Composite membranes show enhanced water uptake, ion exchange capacity, and mechanical stability compared to that of pristine membrane. Interestingly, to address the environmental impact of acids present in industrial waste, AEMs are employed for acid recovery from model waste at higher concentrations of acid and salt using diffusion dialysis. For prepared membranes, 35.5–47.5% acid recovery has been achieved in presence of metal ions. SCE-1.0 (1.0 wt % GQD/ QPSU) membrane shows the highest acid recovery (47.5%) among the membranes. Composite membranes suggest their potential utility in acidic effluent treatment via diffusion dialysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Anion exchange membrane
Quantum dots
Diffusion dialysis
Acid recovery
Separation factor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and membrane preparation
Characterization of GQDs and composite membranes

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: CouncilÂ ofÂ Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Board of Research of Nuclear Sciences
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: V. Kulshrestha is thankful to Board of Research of Nuclear Sciences (BRNS), Mumbai for providing financial support to carry out the work. VY highly acknowledges CSIR, India for senior research fellowship (SRF). Analytical and Environmental Science Division and Centralized Instrument Facility, CSIR-CSMCRI, Bhavnagar is greatly acknowledged for instrumental support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of GQDs at different magnifications.
1H NMR spectra of polysulfone (PSU), chloromethylated polysulfone (CMPSU), and quaternized polysulfone (QPSU).
Atomic force microscopy images with roughness profile for pristine QPSU (a) and 1.0 wt % QD/QPSU composite membrane (b).
Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis of pristine and composite membranes.
Mechanical properties of pristine and composite membranes in the hydrated state.
Variation in membrane ionic conductivity with temperature.
Acid recovery for pristine and composite membranes (Initial HCl concentration in retentate compartment are 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 2.0 M, and 3.0 M while, DI water was used in diffusate compartment).

Fe+2 leakage through synthesized membranes at 30 °C. (10 g/L, 20 g/L, and 30 g/L in 0.1 M HCl feed while 0.1 M HCl in diffusate compartment and 10 g/L, 20 g/L, and 30 g/L of FeCl2 in 2 M HCl solution feed while de-ionized water in diffusate compartment).
Acid recovery for prepared membranes in 2 M HCl solution with varying FeCl2 concentrations in diffusion experiment. (Diffusate compartment filled with de-ionized water).
Acid recovery for SCE-1.0 membrane with and without FeCl2 in 2 M HCl.

Study of proton diffusion coefficients (UH +) and separation factor (S) for pristine and composite membranes with an initial concentration of (a)10 g/L FeCl2, (b) 20 g/L FeCl2 and (c) 30 g/L FeCl2 in 2 M HCl.
Effect of reaction parameters on chloromethylation of polysulfone.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Tethered CH2Cl was determined by 1H NMR.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Cross-linking was measured by simple weighing.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Water uptake (φ), ion exchange capacity (IEC), hydration number (λ), ionic conductivity (σ), activation energy (Ea), and linear swelling ratio of pristine and composite membranes.
Comparison of the present study results with some of the published literatures.

head.ce:title: Polysulfone/graphene quantum dots composite anion exchange membrane for acid recovery by diffusion dialysis

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. CSIR-Central Salt & Marine Chemicals Research Institute (CSIR-CSMCRI), Gijubhai Badheka Marg, Bhavnagar 364002, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Central Salt & Marine Chemicals Research Institute (CSIR-CSMCRI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The advancement in membrane-based separation technologies offers advantages over traditional ways. Recovery of acid from acidic waste stream is an important issue to be solved by greener route. Diffusion dialysis using anion exchange membranes (AEMs) is an energy-intensive and environment friendly process to be taken forward. Herein, we report the eco-friendly route for chloromethylation of polysulfone using chloromethyl ethyl ether (CMEE). Effect of chloromethyl ethyl ether concentration, reaction temperature, and reaction time are investigated to get maximum tethered chloromethyl group and high yield of product. Series of chloromethylated polysulfone membranes are prepared with different concentrations of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) followed by quaternization using trimethylamine. The prepared membranes are characterized by their water content, transport properties, and stabilities. Composite membranes show enhanced water uptake, ion exchange capacity, and mechanical stability compared to that of pristine membrane. Interestingly, to address the environmental impact of acids present in industrial waste, AEMs are employed for acid recovery from model waste at higher concentrations of acid and salt using diffusion dialysis. For prepared membranes, 35.5–47.5% acid recovery has been achieved in presence of metal ions. SCE-1.0 (1.0 wt % GQD/ QPSU) membrane shows the highest acid recovery (47.5%) among the membranes. Composite membranes suggest their potential utility in acidic effluent treatment via diffusion dialysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Environmental pollution is continually increasing by rapid industrial development. Electroplating, paper milling, metal refining, steel processing, hydrometallurgy, alkaloid production, and many more industries are involved in discharge of huge amounts of toxic metal ion-containing acidic waste which impose severe environmental pollution [1–4]. In recent past, various processes have been employed to treat industrial acidic effluent namely solvent extraction, thermal decomposition, crystallization, neutralization, and distillation, and are effective in addressing the above issue but regrettably, high energy consumption and high capital cost limit their implementation [1,3–6]. Nowadays, membrane-based technologies such as electrodialysis (ED), ultrafiltration (UF), reverse osmosis (RO), and diffusion dialysis (DD) have been widely applied to address the issues related to separation and purification [7,8]. Among these, diffusion dialysis has drawn immense attention in industrial effluent treatment and has been considered as an attractive technology for separation and concentration of sulfuric acid, hydrochloric acid, nitric acid, and mixed acids in an economically viable manner under concentration gradient driving force [1,2,5,9–11]. Besides, diffusion dialysis has several advantages over others such as its low cost, low energy consumption, environment-friendliness, and easy operation [1–5]. Moreover, DD is a thermodynamically favorable process as it involves an increase in entropy and a decrease in over-all Gibbs free energy of the system [2]. Typically, fixed cationic moieties tethered in AEMs offer facile anionic migration towards lower concentration side in the DD process. Meanwhile, counter-ion (H+ or M+n) accompanied by anion also transported to balance electro-neutrality, wherein counter-ions with small hydrated radii, lower valance charge, and high mobility are preferred over other [12–16]. Although, the reclamation of high purity acid in the DD process is caught in a dilemma. Thus, the "trade-off" effect between the proton diffusion coefficient and H+/M+n selectivity is the bottleneck in AEMs for diffusion dialysis.

Therefore, to beat the effect between proton diffusion coefficients and H+/M+n selectivity, enormous researches have been strived in recent years [4,12,13,16]. An AEM for DD must have reasonable water uptake, high anion permeability and selectivity, good thermo-mechanical stability, and excellent chemical resistance along with cost-effectiveness. Considerable amount of literature have been published for the synthesis of anion exchange membranes based on polyvinyl alcohol [1,3,5,8,14,15], polyaniline [9,17], poly(phenylene oxide) [10,12,13,16], poly(aromatic ether sulfone) [4], poly(vinylidene fluoride-co-hexafluoro propylene) [18]. Polysulfone (PSU), a high-performance thermoplastic engineering polymer could be a good candidate for the synthesis of anion exchange membrane for diffusion dialysis. PSU has good solubility in most polar solvents, good thermo-mechanical stability, and excellent chemical stability over a wide range of pH [19,20]. Usually, polysulfone is chloromethylated using chloromethyl methyl ether to introduce functional groups [21–23]. Although chloromethyl methyl ether is suspected to mild carcinogenic and during its commercial preparation, it is usually contaminated with a small content of bis (chloromethyl) ether (BCME) which is considered as a strong carcinogenic compound [24,25]. To address health issues associated with the use of chloromethyl methyl ether, we describe an alternate approach for chloromethylation of polysulfone using chloromethyl ethyl ether. Carbon-based additives have attracted considerable attention due to their excellent mechanical, thermal, and chemical stability [26,27]. Despite astonishing properties, poor dispersion of carbon-based additives constrained their further processing. However, designing of functionally compatible additive is critical for the use as an additive. Therefore, fine-tuning of these materials via chemical functionalization makes them compatible with a wide variety of solvents and for stable composite membranes. On top of that, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with possible oxygenated functionalities have been synthesized. It has been witnessed that additives with enriched oxygenated functionalities provide hydrogen-bonded channels for facile ionic transport [13,28,29].

In view of with the use of graphene quantum dots and chloromethyl ethyl ether, herein we present graphene quantum dot/quaternized polysulfone (GQD/QPSU) based composite anion exchange membranes choosing polysulfone as a backbone and investigated the effect of concentration of chloromethyl ethyl ether, reaction temperature, and reaction time on chloromethylation of polysulfone. We also study the effect of graphene quantum dots on membrane transport and mechanical properties. Herein, the effect of salt concentration on acid recovery, salt leakage, and change in volume with time was studied during the diffusion dialysis experiment.

Polysulfone pellets with average molecular weight ~35,000 and tin (IV) chloride were obtained from Sigma Aldrich. Multiwalled carbon nanotubes were procured from TCI. Other chemicals of laboratory reagent (LR) grade were procured from local suppliers. De-ionized water was used throughout the experiments.

Chloromethyl ethyl ether was synthesized akin to the procedure reported by Yadav et al. and considered for chloromethylation of polysulfone in the current study [30]. In a two neck round bottom flask, 5 g polysulfone was dissolved in 100 mL of chloroform to make a homogenous solution, wherein anhydrous tin (IV) chloride (2 wt % in relation to reactants) as a Lewis acid catalyst was added dropwise and allowed to mix for half an hour. The desired amount of chloromethyl ethyl ether was added dropwise in the solution and refluxed at a temperature to take a reaction for various hours (Table 1 ). After cooling, the resulting solution was precipitated in methanol and then repeatedly washed with methanol (three-fold), followed by vacuum drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots and membrane preparation
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were synthesized by chemical oxidation of MWCNTs following the procedure reported in the literature with slight modifications [11,31]. Briefly, 500 mg of MWCNT was treated with 160 mL mixture of H2SO4 and HNO3 (2:3 V/V) at 160 °C for 24 h and subsequently reaction mixture allowed to cool followed by washing with copious amount of de-ionized water until neutral pH. Further, 100 mL aqueous solution of oxidized MWCNT subjected to hydrothermal reaction to transform the carboxylic group into amide group by a simple reaction with ammonium acetate the at 180 °C for 24 h, wherein 0.5 M K2SO4 used as a defragmentation agent. Then the solution was centrifuged to remove large particles, and supernatant with GQD was collected.

For the synthesis of GQD/QPSU composite anion exchange membranes, an amount of GQD was dispersed in DMAc and mixed with 20 wt % separately prepared homogenous solution of chloromethylated polysulfone and further stirred for 8 h at room temperature followed by ultra-sonication. The resultant solution was cast on a glass plate using a doctor's blade to get the desired thickness followed by vacuum drying for 24 h at 50 °C. Dried membranes were peeled off and placed in the aqueous solution of trimethylamine at 40 °C for 24 h for successive quaternization of chloromethyl group of polysulfone by menshutkin reaction between chloromethyl group and tertiary amine. Quaternized anion exchange membranes were further washed with a huge amount of water and stored in de-ionized water. To evaluate the effect of GQD on membrane properties, the amount of GQD varied with different wt. % ratios (0.1, 0.5, and 1.0 wt %) with respect to chloromethylated polysulfone and prepared membranes designated as SCE-0.1, SCE-0.5, and SCE-1.0, respectively. Membrane without GQD is designated as SCE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and composite membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The structure, functional group, and morphology of synthesized GQDs and composite membranes were thoroughly characterized by using various analytical techniques. The chemical structure for chloromethylated polysulfone and membranes has been confirmed by 1H NMR using CDCl3, and DMSO‑d 6 solvents, respectively, and recorded using JEOL RESONANCE ECZ600R spectrometer. The functional group analysis of GQDs along with membranes (Fourier-transform infrared-attenuated total reflectance method) carried out recording their FT-IR spectra on PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer. UV–visible spectra of GQD was recorded on Varian (Carry-500) UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer while size and shape were evaluated by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (JEOL, JEM 2100). Membrane surface roughness and topology were visualized in semi-contact mode using atomic force microscope (AFM) instrument (Ntegra Aura, NT-MDT, Moscow). Membrane surface morphology was analyzed on field-emission electron microscope (FE-SEM) using JEOL JEM 7100F, USA. Thermal and mechanical stabilities of the composite membranes were analyzed by thermogravimetric (TG) analysis at a heating rate of 10 °C/min from 30 °C to 600 °C under N2 atmosphere (NETZSCH TG 209F1 Libra) and universal testing machine (UTM) (ZWICK ROELL BT-FR2.5TH.40) at 30 °C, respectively.

Physicochemical characterizations including water uptake (φ), dimensional change (DC), hydration number (λ), ion exchange capacity (IEC), and ionic conductivity (σ) of the membranes were measured using standard techniques and details are given in ESI section.

The diffusion dialysis experiment was performed using in a house built bicompartment diffusion cell (70 mL capacity of each compartment) in batch mode at 30 °C. In the DD experiment, a test membrane sample of 12.56 cm2 effective area is clamped between two compartments and solutions in both compartments continuously stirred to conquer concentration polarization. For acid recivery, feed retentate compartment (Cret) is filled with different HCl concentrations and another diffusate compartment (Cdiff) with the same volume of de-ionized water. To investigate the effect of FeCl2 on acid recovery, feed retentate compartment (Cret) is filled with different concentration of FeCl2 and another diffusate compartment (Cdiff) with DI water. Leakage of Fe+2 during diffusion dialysis was studied with 0.1 M HCl in both the compartment. The diffusion experiments were carried out for 8 h. Concentration difference in both compartments leads to acid diffusion from Cret to Cdiff and was measured at a regular interval of 1 h by acid-base titration. Inductively coupled plasma spectrometry was used for the analysis of metal ion estimation. Parameters such as metal ion leakage, acid recovery, and volume change during diffusion experiment in relation to diffusion time were also calculated using the given formulae [32]:

Fe+2, diff is the volume of solution in diffusate compartment, concentration of HCl in diffusate compartment, and concentration of Fe+2 ions in diffusate compartment, respectively. Whereas Vret, CHCl, ret, and CFe+2, ret are the volume of solution, concentration of HCl, and Fe+2 in retentate compartment, respectively. The initial time and change in time for DD experiments are represented by t
in and Δt, respectively.

The dialysis coefficient (U) for proton and metal ion was calculated using the following relation:
(4)

=
M
A t Δ C
Where, M represents the amount of H+ or Fe+2 transported from retentate to diffusate in moles, A is the effective area of test membrane samples exposed for diffusion (m2), t is diffusion time (h) and ΔC is a logarithmic average concentration between two compartments (mol/m3), calculated using following relation [9,17]:
(5)

Where C

ret are the concentration of the solution in retentate compartment at zero time and t time, respectively. Ct diff is the concentration of solution in diffusate at time t.

Separation factor (S) is the ratio of dialysis coefficients (U) of two species present in solutions and calculated using the given equation:
(6)
S
=
U H
U
F e

UV–visible spectrum for an aqueous dispersion of GQDs is displayed in Fig. S1, wherein two weak bands are observed at 207 nm and 260 nm coupled with π–π* transitions of –CC- skeleton and n–π* transition of –CO group, respectively. These findings are in good agreement with previously reported literature [33]. The IR spectrum of GQDs shows the characteristic absorption band at 1592 cm−1 associated with –CC characteristics of polycyclic aromatic compound (Fig. S1 (inset)). In addition to this new absorption band at 1151 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 assigned for stretching vibrations of –C-O and –CO, respectively [33,34]. The broad absorption band at 3420 cm−1 shows the presence of –OH group. Morphology and size of GQDs are analyzed by TEM imaging and corresponding images are presented in Fig. 1 . From images, spherical shape GQDs of size 2–10 nm visualized clearly.

Chloromethylation reaction on polysulfone is an electrophilic substitution reaction and occurs on phenyl ring adjacent to the isopropyl group, instead of those ring present near sulfone group. Chloromethylation reaction could easily cause gel formation through cross-linking reaction between active phenyl ring and chloromethyl group of another chain in Friedel-crafts alkylation manner, resulting in inter or intra-polymer architecture. This will eventually reduce the overall yield of the chloromethylated product. To address the above, we studied the effect of concentration of chloromethylating reagent, reaction temperature, and time on chloromethylation of polysulfone.

The number of tethered chloromethyl group were calculated using 1H NMR spectroscopy by integrating the peak area of the chloromethyl group in relation to the isopropyl group using the following relation:
(7)
- CH
2
Cl
tethered
=
3
×
A
C
H 2
C l
A
C
(
C
H 3
)
2
Where ACH2Cl is the peak area of –CH2Cl protons in the vicinity of 4.4–4.6 ppm and AC(CH3)2 corresponds to the peak area of proton associated with isopropyl group assigned at 1.6–1.8 ppm in the 1H NMR spectrum. Gel formation was determined by simply weighing the dried chloromethylated product.

To minimize cross-linking, a sufficient amount of CMEE should be present in reaction medium with respect to polysulfone, to react with reactive phenyl rings thus minimizing the possibility of Friedel-crafts alkylation reaction. The effect of CMEE concentration on chloromethylation of polysulfone was studied at 70 °C reaction temperature, 60 min reaction time, and 2 wt % catalyst in relation to reactants. With the increase in CMEE amount starting from 0.4 wt % to 2 wt %, the number of tethered chloromethyl sites increase from 0.01 to 1.23 per repeating unit, respectively (Table 1). In contrast, further increase in CMEE amount results in a serious reduction in the number of chloromethyl groups, wherein enhancing number of active phenyl rings would leads to gel formation. Fig. S2(a) represents the 1H NMR spectra of chloromethylated polysulfone which enlightens that peak intensity of protons associated with chloromethyl group increases significantly with the increase in CMEE amount. Elevated temperature either accelerate the reaction or some of the unnecessary side reactions are initiated which are avoided at low temperature. The effect of temperature for chloromethylation reaction was studied from 30 °C to 90 °C, taking constant reaction time 60 min and chloromethyl ethyl ether concentration (2 wt %). Table 1 and Fig. S2(b) implies that elevated temperature promotes the chloromethylation reaction but also responsible for cross-linking. As we increase the temperature from 30 °C to 90 °C, the number of tethered –CH2Cl increased from 0.04 to 1.49 but at 90 °C yield of chloromethylated products reduced by 21%. In this study, 70 °C reaction temperature found to be optimum to get the highest yield with maximum reactive –CH2Cl group.

Effect of reaction time at 70 °C temperature and 2 wt % chloromethyl ethyl ether concentration on chloromethylation reaction is studied and presented in Table 1 and Fig. S2(c)). Surprisingly, both number of chloromethyl groups and cross-linking of polymer increase up to 120 min reaction time. While, further increase in reaction time results in 23% cross-linking along with lower –CH2Cl sites. From the results, it is presumed that 60 min reaction time is good enough to get a sufficient number of chloromethyl groups with optimum yield. In current study 2 wt % chloromethyl ethyl ether in relation to polysulfone, 70 °C reaction temperature, and 60 min reaction time are speculated as utmost parameters for chloromethylation of polysulfone to get the highest –CH2Cl group with remarkable yield.

The chemical structure of neat polysulfone (PSU), chloromethylated polysulfone (CMPSU), and qauternized polysulfone (QPSU) are revealed by 1H NMR study and concordant spectra shown in Fig. 2 . The characteristic chemical shifts in the vicinity of δ = 1.7 ppm assigned to –CH3 group of isopropyl chain present in PSU, CMPSU, and QPSU. While, CMPSU exhibits an additional chemical shift at δ = 4.54 ppm, which is assigned to protons of the chloromethyl group [35,36]. On the other hand, new chemical shift at δ = 3.05 ppm assigned for –CH3 group linked to quaternary nitrogen and a broad chemical shift δ = 4.5 ppm assigned to –CH2 group linked with quaternary nitrogen from one side and on another side by an aromatic ring, confirms the successful quaternization of chloromethylated polysulfone [37]. The FTIR-ATR spectra for membranes presented in Fig. S3, wherein characteristic absorption bands of polysulfone observed at 1590 cm−1, 1480 cm−1, 1240 cm−1, 1145 cm−1, and 1018 cm−1 are assigned to the –CC skeletal vibrations of aromatic hydrocarbon, 1,4-aromatic ring substitution vibrations, antisymmetric vibrations of ether linkage, symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibration of OSO, respectively [36,38]. In addition to these, absorption bands centered at 1386 cm−1 and 1652 cm−1 are assigned to –CH2N+(CH3)3 stretching vibrations originated from the quaternization reaction of chloromethylated polysulfone with trimethylamine [20,39]. Importantly, absorption bands located in a region of 2800–3000 cm−1 are ascribed to stretching vibrations of –C-H bond associated with isopropyl and trimethyl groups. The obtained results are in good agreement with 1H NMR results and confirm the successful quaternization of chloromethylated polysulfone.

The surface and cross-sectional morphologies of pristine and composite membrane are analyzed by SEM imaging at different magnifications and images are displayed in Fig. S4. It can be seen that surface and cross-section of pristine and composite membranes are dense, non-porous, and flawless. It is realized that bare membrane exhibits smooth morphology, in contrast, a rough morphology is observed for composite membranes due to fine dispersion of GQD particles with better compatibility with polymer matrix (Fig. S4 E and G). Besides, cross-sectional images enlighten that GQD particles are uniformly embedded in PSU matrix in addition to their presence on the surface. Fig. S5 shows the captured optical images for pristine QPSU and 0.5 wt % GQD composite in visible and ultra-violet light. The images are in good agreement with homogenous nature as well as even distribution of GQDs within QPSU matrix. The change in surface topology and roughness for pristine QPSU and 1.0 wt % composite membrane is analyzed by AFM imaging and presented in Fig. 3 . Pristine membrane (Fig. 3a) showed smooth morphology, in contrast, rough morphology with uniformly distributed GQDs throughout the membrane visualized in SCE-1.0 composite membrane (Fig. 3b). The phase images showed dark and light regions and are assigned to the hydrophobic polymer backbone and hydrophilic ionic domains, respectively [40]. Average roughness and root mean squire roughness for SCE-1.0 found to be 6.15 and 7.47 nm, respectively, whereas for SCE the same is 2.29 and 2.99 nm.

As apparent from TGA and DTGA in Fig. 4 , pristine and composite membranes exhibit four-step degradation profile. The first step degradation commenced with the removal of absorbed water molecules below 100 °C for pristine QPSU and its composites. The second step loss in a temperature range of 140–240 °C is due to the removal of water molecules present in close proximity with fixed quaternary ammonium moieties and partial degradation of quaternary ammonium group [19,41]. Surprisingly, second step weight loss for composite membranes is remarkably higher in relation to pristine QPSU due to presence of oxygenated functionalities enriched GQDs which serve as subsidiary functional groups to hold up water molecules utilizing hydrogen-bonded network. The third step degradation around 260–330 °C is associated with the complete degradation of quaternary ammonium groups. The last step loss beyond 400 °C is due to decomposition of polymer skeleton [22,41].

Tensile strength and elongation at break are evaluated in wet conditions to testify the mechanical stability of prepared membranes. As evident from Fig. 5 , composite membranes display considerably high tensile strength and elongation at break in comparison to pristine QPSU membrane. The tensile strength and elongation at break for 1.0 wt % composite membranes are 37% and 28% higher than 8.22 MPa and 60.34% achieved for pristine QPSU membrane. From above observations, it is envisaged that a small amount of GQD reduced the free volume of the membrane matrix by strong interfacial and hydrogen bonding interactions with polysulfone chains, thereby offering high mechanical stability to the composite membranes [12].

Water uptake (WU) and IEC are the two decisive parameters, which govern the overall DD performance of the membrane. It is well documented that, WU is pivotal in ionic transport but excessive water uptake causes impermissible dimensional changes and impairs the mechanical strength [42,43]. As shown in Table 2 , the water uptake for composite membranes is significantly higher than their pristine counterpart. An increase in water uptake value from 24.73% to 32.12% is realized with an increase in GQD content from 0.1 wt % to 1.0 wt % for the membranes with the same degree of functionalization of quaternary ammonium group. The higher water uptake value for composite membranes is attributed to the presence of covalently bonded hydroxyl and carboxylic acid functionalities over GQD. The –OH and –COOH groups serve as subordinate functional groups for water uptake by forming hydrogen-bonded interactions [12]. The ion exchange capacity of prepared membranes is tabulated in Table 2 and found in a range of 1.52–1.81 meq/g. The high IEC for composite membranes allows more Cl- ions to be transported in diffusate compartment; consequently, acid recovery speeded up by transport of small-sized H+ accompanied by Cl- ions to maintain electro-neutrality in diffusate compartment [3,8]. To better understand the effect of GQD in composite membranes, hydration numbers have been calculated and listed in Table 2. A higher λ value is observed for composite membrane in comparison to their pristine counterpart and λ value increases with an increase in GQD content. Dimensional changes for GQD/QPSU composite membranes are higher than bare QPSU membrane and is also increases as the content on GQD increases in membrane matrix (Table 2). This can be evidenced by the higher water uptake of composite membranes and results are consistent with the previous report [12]. However, membranes were found to be stable without compromising the other properties.

As obvious from Table 2, composite membranes reveal high ionic conductivity, wherein GQDs provide the interconnected path for the transport of ions in the membrane matrix. The ionic conductivity exhibited by 0.1 wt % GQD/QPSU composite membrane is 1.48 × 10−2 S/cm that is 32% higher than 1.12 × 10−2 S/cm obtained for pristine QPSU membrane. Ionic conductivity of AEMs strongly relies on ion exchange capacity and water content within the membrane matrix [44]. Hydrophilic GQD attracts more water molecules that are integrated with quaternary ammonium domains provides facile ionic mobility within membrane. Effect of temperature on ionic conductivity was also studied, as the temperature increases the mobility of ions increases and resultant enhancement in conductivity. Ionic conductivity of all the membranes found to increase with the temperature (Fig. 6 ). Ionic conductivity results for membranes are in good agreement with IEC and water uptake, and this could be evidenced by an increased number of hydrophilic moieties in composite membranes. All the membranes exhibit an Arrhenius type of thermal dependence on ionic conductivity (Fig. S6). The amount of energy required for ionic transport in membrane matrix was calculated and presented in Table 2.

Recovery of acid from the feed of different acid concentrations (0.5 M, 1 M, 2 M, and 3 M) with time was studied at 30 °C and presented in Fig. S-7 (a-d). The acid concentration in diffusate compartment increases as diffusion time increases. It is noticeable that initially acid concentration in diffusate compartment increase rapidly due to concentration gradient driving force and then almost steady with time. This can be evidenced, according to Fick's law, which relates the diffusion to concentration gradient across the membrane in two chambers separated by AEM and diffusion decreases significantly along time due to reduction in concentration in feed solution [45]. Higher diffusion flux was obtained for composite membranes in comparison with pristine membrane due to their excellent water content. The high IEC values for composite membranes allow more Cl- ions to be transported in the diffusate compartment; consequently, transport of H+ ions speeded up to maintain electro-neutrality in diffusate compartment [8,46]. On the other hand, high WU of composite membranes results in a significantly higher concentration of H+ ions in membrane matrix, thereby generating a concentration gradient between AEM matrix and diffusate compartment. Hence, transport of H+ ions accelerated, as a result, higher diffusion flux is obtained for composite membranes [1,8]. However, acid permeability values strongly influenced by acid concentration in feed solution and it is to be noted that acid diffusion increases as the concentration of acid increases in the retentate compartment. The maximum concentration of recovered acid in diffusate side for SCE-1.0 is 1.33 M, whereas for pristine QPSU it is 0.9 M at the same time for 3 M feed solution. Acid recovery for pristine and composite membranes in diffusion dialysis is presented in Fig. 7 . Highest acid recovery for SCE membrane with 0.5 M, 1.0 M, 2.0 M, and 3.0 M HCl are 18%, 23%, 26%, and 30%, respectively whereas for SCE-1.0 it is 28%, 37%, 42%, and 44%, respectively. Though, small size proton in the form of hydronium ion (H3O+) transported accompanying chloride ion to accomplish the principle of electro-neutrality. Consequently, change in solution volume for both the compartments is noticeable. In the case of very low acid concentration (0.5 M) volume change is negligible, while marginal volume change is realized at higher concentrations (1 M–3 M) due to electro-osmotic drag from feed to permeate side as shown in Fig. S7 (b-d) [32,45,47].

Iron leakage across the membranes was also evaluated using different iron concentrations (10 g/L, 20 g/L, and 30 g/L) in 0.1 M HCl retentate compartment, meanwhile same acid concentration was used in diffusate compartment to maintain acidic pH in both compartments. Fe+2 leakage for composite membranes is higher than that of pristine membrane and increases with the increase in GQD content in membrane matrix, as shown in Fig. S8 (a-c). As presented in Fig. S8 (a-c), iron leakage increases with increase in iron concentration in feed due to concentration gradient across two compartments. The highest 8.43% iron leakage was observed with highest iron concentration (30 g/L FeCl2) in feed for SCE-1.0 composite membrane (Fig. 8 ). Volume difference associated with water flux in retentate and diffusate compartment is shown in Fig. S8 (a-c). As reported in Fig. S8, an increase in volume of retentate compartment is governed by osmotic pressure as driving force from diffusate compartment to retentate one [45,48,49].

The acid reclamation studies for prepared membranes using 2 M HCl with varying concentration of FeCl2 (10 g/L, 20 g/L, and 30 g/L) has been performed at 30 °C and data presented in Fig. 9 . Figs. 7 and 9 reflect that membranes exhibit high acid recovery in presence of FeCl2 rather than its absence in acidic solution. The highest acid recovery of 47.5% and 44% is realized for membranes using 2 M HCl feed with and without FeCl2 in solution, respectively. This could be explained based on the “salt effect”. The addition of salt to the solution with the same counter anion of acid results in additional driving force for the passage of anion, wherein to maintain electro-neutrality of the system transport of proton across anion exchange membrane becomes more facile [6]. Additionally, acid recovery for the membranes increases with the increase in FeCl2 content in solution at constant acid concentration (Fig. 9). It is believed that iron leakage accompanied by acid permeation in diffusate chamber [6]. Apparent from, Fig. 8, marginal increase in Fe+2 leakage is noticed with the increase in FeCl2 amount from 10 g/L to 30 g/L at constant HCl concentration for the prepared membranes. But a noticeable increase in Fe+2 leakage is observed with the increase in GQD amount in composite membranes. The presence of –OH and –COOH on GQDs in composite membrane quickens the transport of H+ and Fe+2 ions, but Fe+2 transported at lower rate due to larger ionic radius as well as high valance state. The acid recovery as a function of diffusion time with and without FeCl2 in 2 M HCl feed for SCE-1.0 composite membrane is presented in Fig. 10 . There is no significant volume change was observed during these experiments as osmotic drag and electro-osmotic drag oppose each other during the process.

The diffusion coefficients and ion discrimination for membranes are studied to analyze their potential in the DD process. Fig. 11 (a–c) displays the proton diffusion coefficient (U
H
+) and separation factor (S) for prepared membranes at constant 2 M HCl feed concentration with varying FeCl2 amount. The U
H
+ values for composite membranes are higher than pristine QPSU membrane. The SCE-1.0 composite membrane exhibit U
H
+ of 4.92 × 10−3 mh−1, 5.19 × 10−3 mh−1, and 5.73 × 10−3 mh−1 for 10 g/L, 20 g/L, and 30 g/L FeCl2 in 2 M HCl solution, respectively which are remarkably higher than 2.48 × 10−3 mh−1, 2.73 × 10−3 mh−1, and 3.41 × 10−3 mh−1 exhibited by pristine QPSU membrane under similar operating conditions. The results obtained for SCE-1.0 composite membranes are more promising compared to that of DF-120B used commercially [50]. The high U
H
+ values accounted for composite membranes explained based on more facile proton migration in composite membranes due to presence of oxygenated functionalities enriched graphene quantum dots in composite membranes. It is well documented that –OH group forms strong hydrogen-bonded network with water molecules which ultimately makes the AEM matrix more pleasant for fast H+ transport [1,5,8,14,50]. Besides, a carboxylic acid group is considered as advantageous in proton transport due to competence in ion exchange as well as hydrogen bonding with protons along with water molecules. Although carboxylic acid has lower ionization coefficient, despite that it offers additional sites for water molecules to form hydrogen-bonded network and proton transport channels [13,28,29]. Also, it has been hypothesized that –COOH group in AEMs favors the transport of H+ ions over multivalent metal cations [13].

The separation factor (S) is the ratio of U
H
+ relative to U
Fe +2. Fig. 11(a–c) shows that the S values for membranes found to be increasing with the increase in FeCl2 in feed solution. As discussed above proton transport is boosted by an increase in FeCl2 amount at constant acid concentration. The lower S values are observed for composite membranes, even though they possess high proton diffusion coefficients and S values for composite membranes decrease with an increase in GQD content in polymer matrix. It has been observed that both U
H
+ and U
Fe
+2 values increase with an increase in GQD content, this is presumably due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic acid groups, which are contributing in the formation of strong hydrogen-bonded network. The hydrogen-bonding interactions are quite enough stronger to shorten the inter-atomic distance affirmative for ionic transport [3,15]. Therefore, transport of H+ and Fe+2 ions accelerated, but Fe+2 ions transported at a lower rate due to larger ionic radius as well as high valance state, in this way selectivity for composite membranes has been compromised marginally. Additionally, it is well documented that water uptake is essential for ionic transport, besides higher WU exhibit higher permeability for Fe+2 ions too, hence a contrary trend in selectivity is observed for composite membranes [1,3,16].

Similar to the current study, lower selectivity at higher proton diffusion coefficient values were reported in many literatures [1,3,5,8,16,51–53]. The S values for SCE-1.0 composite membrane are in the range of 32–39, which is comparable to previous reports and significantly higher than commercial DF-120B membrane (S = 20) as shown in Table 3 [50]. Results show that the SCE-1.0 membrane can be potentially employed to serve the purpose of acid recovery from the acidic waste.

In summary, this work suggests the possibility of acid recovery from effluent in high-level acidic waste from different industries. GQD/QPSU composite anion exchange membranes have been synthesized for acid recovery by diffusion dialysis. Prepared membranes have high water content (22.26–32.12%) in matrix essential for proton carriage by hoping mechanism, excellent IEC (1.52–1.81 meq/g), and dense nature to restrict passage of metal ion. Composite membranes showed their potential in acid recovery from acidic effluent in presence of metal ions. Eventually, the proton diffusion coefficient and separation factor for prepared membranes are also found in good agreement with the literature and membrane has the potential for acid recovery from acidic effluent.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The manuscript declares no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: V. Kulshrestha is thankful to Board of Research of Nuclear Sciences (BRNS), Mumbai for providing financial support to carry out the work. VY highly acknowledges CSIR, India for senior research fellowship (SRF). Analytical and Environmental Science Division and Centralized Instrument Facility, CSIR-CSMCRI, Bhavnagar is greatly acknowledged for instrumental support.

Xu, Improved acid recovery performance by novel Poly(DMAEM-co-γ-MPS) anion exchange membrane via diffusion dialysis, Journal of Membrane Science 525 (2017) 163-174.
